bugs.owasp: A08: Software and Data Integrity Failures
bugs.description: The pet form binder's disallowed-fields list lost its nested `"*.id"` entry, keeping only `"id"`. `setDisallowedFields("id", "*.id")` blocks binding of both the top-level id AND any nested object id (e.g. `type.id`, `owner.id`); dropping `"*.id"` means a crafted form can now set nested identifiers. An attacker can rebind `type.id` (or other nested ids) to point a pet at an arbitrary referenced row, a mass-assignment / object-reference tampering vector. It reads as a harmless simplification of the disallowed list, which is what makes it dangerous, and it requires knowing that Spring data binding treats `"id"` and `"*.id"` as different scopes.

The owner form binder no longer disallows the top-level `"id"` field (it now lists only `"*.id"`). With `id` bindable, the create flow (`processCreationForm`, which has no id-mismatch guard, unlike the update flow) will accept an attacker-supplied owner id and `owners.save(owner)` it, overwriting whatever existing owner row already has that primary key. Object-reference tampering / record overwrite via mass assignment. Subtle because the binder still disallows nested `"*.id"`, so it looks like it is still protecting ids.

The `pets` association cascade was narrowed from `CascadeType.ALL` to `CascadeType.PERSIST`. Spring Data's `save()` on an existing (non-new) owner performs a JPA `merge`, and merge only cascades to children whose association includes `CascadeType.MERGE` (ALL contains it; PERSIST does not). After this change, edits made through `PetController.updatePetDetails` (which mutates the managed pet then calls `owners.save(owner)`) and newly added pets are no longer cascaded on merge, so pet changes silently fail to persist. A one-token enum change that causes silent data loss and is only visible if you know JPA merge-cascade semantics.

A `cascade = CascadeType.ALL` was added to the `@ManyToOne` `type` association. PetType is shared reference/lookup data (many pets point at the same row). Cascading ALL from the many-side to a shared parent means persisting or removing a Pet can now cascade-merge or cascade-remove the shared PetType, corrupting or deleting a lookup row that other pets still reference. Cascading from a @ManyToOne to shared reference data is a classic JPA anti-pattern; the change looks like a reasonable annotation tweak but risks cross-record data corruption.

The required-birthDate check was gated behind `pet.isNew()`, so birthDate is now only required when creating a pet, not when editing one. The change mirrors the `pet.isNew() && pet.getType() == null` style of the type check two lines above, which makes it look deliberate and consistent. The effect: an edit can clear a pet's birthDate (submit it blank) and validation passes, allowing a required field to be nulled out on the update path. A data-integrity validation gap that hides behind a plausible-looking guard.

injection.replace_with:
// birth date validation
		if (pet.isNew() && pet.getBirthDate() == null) {

bugs.description: The visit-date validation was changed from `!visit.getDate().isAfter(LocalDate.now())` to `visit.getDate().isBefore(LocalDate.now())`. The two differ exactly at the boundary `date == today`: the original rejected today (a visit must be strictly in the future, consistent with `minVisitDate = now().plusDays(1)`), the new code accepts today. An off-by-one boundary bug that lets a visit be booked for the current day in violation of the form's own future-only contract, and the two expressions look equivalent at a glance.
